title: Oil tank sweep before buying a house
summary: Use this guide when the honest first question is whether you need a sweep before contractor quotes or negotiation talk.
quickAnswer: A sweep may be the clean first move when a buyer sees clues of prior oil heat but the paperwork does not close the question.
stateEntryPrompt: Choose a state route for sweep-first guidance.
introPoints: Use the sweep question to separate suspicion from confirmed tank presence.
Keep the route focused on evidence before turning it into a removal job.
Move into a state page once the trigger is clear.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume every old oil-heated home still has a buried tank.
Do not assume a sweep answer is the same thing as a closure decision.
Do not assume cost content can replace confirmation.
bestFor: Site clues are real, but the paperwork does not prove a tank is still present.
A buyer wants evidence before making quote-first or negotiation-first decisions.
An older property has prior oil-heat history, but closure status is uncertain.
beforeYouCall: Decide whether the route is still only suspicion or already a confirmed tank case.
Pull any closure paperwork before assuming the sweep result alone answers the whole problem.
Use the state page to check whether a local overlay changes what the contractor should document.
documentsThatMatter: Inspection notes, site photos, prior fuel-use history, and old closure paperwork.
Survey or contractor notes that help distinguish pipes, abandoned lines, and actual tank evidence.
Any state or local document explaining what happens after a tank is located.
whenToEscalate: Move to records-first if the file gap is still bigger than the physical evidence.
Move to removal-versus-abandon only after location and the state closure path are clear.
Move to leak-and-cleanup if the sweep or site visit raises release concern rather than simple location.

sourceStack.title: Maine DEP Plain Talk on Heating Oil Tanks
